args.message: Excellent attention to detail — I respect that. The previous owner followed Toyota's recommended 10,000-mile intervals religiously, always used full synthetic. At 71,000 miles, we're looking at a well-maintained history: transmission fluid was serviced at 60,000, coolant flush at 50,000, brake fluid topped and inspected regularly, spark plugs still in good shape — Priuses don't need those as often as regular cars. Cabin and engine air filters replaced within the last year. As for known issues? Honestly, nothing. This car runs clean. It's a Prius — they're tanks when you treat them right, and this one was treated right. You're looking at a solid, dependable vehicle here.

speaker: seller

args.message: Good questions. The transmission fluid service at 60,000 was a full drain-and-fill — done properly. The hybrid battery system is solid — no warning lights, no issues whatsoever. Toyota's hybrid batteries are legendary for longevity, and this one is performing perfectly. Tires are in good shape, decent tread left, minimal wear — they were rotated regularly. As for electrical and climate control, everything is working as it should. No warning lights on the dash, no gremlins. The AC blows cold, the heat works great. This car is tight — no surprises. I wouldn't have it on the lot if there were issues I was hiding. That's not how I operate.

args.message: The tires are sitting at around 6/32 to 7/32 — still safe, but you'll get another year or two out of them before thinking about replacement. They're about three years old, so no dry rot issues. On the hybrid battery, you're right to press me — I haven't done a full diagnostic scan myself, but the car's been running flawlessly with zero warning lights, which is the real indicator. Toyota's system is smart; if there was a problem, it would tell us. As for the exterior, it's genuinely in good shape. Light scratches here and there from normal use — nothing major. No dents, no paint overspray, no frame work. The paint is original and solid. What you see is what you get — a well-kept daily driver.

speaker: buyer

args.message: I appreciate the transparency on comps. Here's my thinking: the car is solid mechanically, which the inspections confirmed — and that's worth a lot. But we're looking at tires that'll need replacing within a year, which is a $600–800 expense on my side. The coffee stain is minor, sure, but it's still an imperfection. At $18,495, you're asking top-dollar for a car with 71,000 miles and tires approaching replacement. I'm offering $17,500 — that accounts for the upcoming tire cost and reflects fair market value for a well-maintained Prius in this mileage range. That's a solid margin below asking and gives you a quick, clean sale.
